Rule Description: A wave of thread blocks is defined as the maximum number of blocks that can be executed in parallel on the target GPU. The number of blocks in a wave depends on the number of multiprocessors and the theoretical occupancy of the kernel. This kernel launch results in 2 full waves and a partial wave of 588 thread blocks. Under the assumption of a uniform execution duration of all thread blocks, this partial wave may account for up to 33.3% of the total runtime of this kernel. Try launching a grid with no partial wave. The overall impact of this tail effect also lessens with the number of full waves executed for a grid. See the Hardware Model (https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/ProfilingGuide/index.html#metrics-hw-model) description for more details on launch configurations.

Under the assumption of a uniform execution duration of all thread blocks, this partial wave may account for up to 33.3% of the total runtime of this kernel. Under the assumption of a uniform execution duration of all thread blocks, this partial wave may account for up to 33.3% of the total runtime of this kernel. Load imbalances can occur between warps within a block as well as across blocks of the same kernel. See the CUDA Best Practices Guide (https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-c-best-practices-guide/index.html#occupancy) for more details on optimizing occupancy.
